arbitrated.notes: The wedge is defined by realised inflation vs. inflation expectations. Inflation (the realised-print driver) is up with fairly high conviction (0.62), but it's 16 days stale relative to a fresh CPI horizon, and per the standing rule a stale expectations read costs more than a stale realised read — here it's the realised leg that's stale, which tempers but doesn't erase it. Inflation expectations, freshly formed this meeting, sits flat at only 0.30 conviction — anchored, not moving. This is close to the textbook signature case: hot/rising realised inflation against anchored expectations. The rule is explicit — resolve toward the expectations driver, not a split. Labor tightness, the cross-read the panel leans on for judging inflation persistence, is also flat (0.35, 23 days old) — it gives no signal of wage-driven persistence that would justify overriding the anchored-expectations read. There's no cross-read here that reframes persistence vs. base effects strongly enough to trigger an override; a single hot inflation print does not qualify per the override rule. So I hold to expectations as the operative driver for the breakeven wedge: anchored, roughly flat. This argues for a narrow/flat breakeven stance rather than a repeat of a widening call, since nothing this meeting shows expectations actually moving. Compared to a plausible prior widening position, the fresh flat expectations read and lack of a persistence-confirming labor signal argue for trimming conviction toward flat/slightly narrow rather than aggressively long breakevens. I'm not moving to a hard short of breakevens because inflation itself is still running hot and could still leak into expectations if persistent, but the anchored read dominates for now.

The relevant drivers here are inflation and inflation_expectations, plus a labor_tightness cross-read for persistence. This meeting is materially different from last time: inflation_expectations, which was previously a near-flat +0.05 (stale/no view), has now formed a fresh, confident +0.65 "up" call. That closes exactly the gap I flagged as my flip condition last meeting — a fresh expectations reading showing a clear upward move. Realised inflation also remains hot (+0.68, up from +0.55, though the read is 13 days old). Labor tightness is flat/anchored (+0.30 conviction, flat direction), which does not confirm a wage-driven persistence story, but it also does not contradict the expectations move — it simply doesn't corroborate a structural inflation story, leaving the wedge argument resting on the direct expectations signal itself.

The key resolution rule here is that when realised inflation is hot and expectations are anchored, you lean on expectations, not a split. But this time expectations are NOT anchored — they've moved up alongside realised inflation, in the same direction, both fresh and confident. That's not the classic divergence case; it's a case of convergence on widening. This meaningfully upgrades my conviction versus last meeting's cautious +0.10 position, which was justified specifically by uncertain/soft expectations. Now that the expectations analyst has flipped from flat to a firm "up," I have a legitimate, panel-driven reason to increase the position size, not just repeat it.

I discount the flat labor_tightness signal as non-corroborating but not disconfirming, and I set aside the curve/term-premium/balance-sheet/financial-conditions analysts as they speak to real yields and duration, not the inflation wedge itself.

Given this, I am reversing my breakeven view from widening to narrowing. The previous long-breakeven position (+0.35) was built on an expectations read that has now flipped; carrying it forward would ignore the explicit falsifier condition I set. I am moving to a modest short-breakeven stance, sized down from prior conviction since the realised-inflation print still creates some tension and the expectations move, while formed at this meeting, is a single read at -0.45, not extreme.</notes>

Last meeting I ran narrower breakevens on hot realised inflation but anchored/falling expectations plus soft labor tightness. This meeting flips the key signal: inflation_expectations, freshly formed, now reads up (+0.35) rather than down, while realised inflation (still the same stale-ish 15-day read) shows down (-0.35) and labor_tightness is flat/stale. Per the framework, when realised and expectations diverge, expectations wins — and here expectations turned. That is exactly the falsifier I set last time: "a return of inflation_expectations to rising/anchored-higher... would flip this back toward widening." That condition is now met, even if only at modest conviction (0.35, freshly formed). Labor stays flat and stale (22 days), giving no strong cross-read either way, so I don't lean on it to override the expectations turn — it's neutral, not confirmatory of tightening.

I read this as a genuine, if not dramatic, reversal: the persistence case for lower breakevens weakens because expectations are no longer accommodating the disinflation story. Realised inflation cooling despite expectations firming is closer to "base effects fading a hot print" than "disinflation entrenching." I close most of the short-breakeven position rather than reverse hard, since conviction on the new expectations print is only moderate (0.35) and inflation itself is still pointing down. Net: move from -0.30 to a small residual short, effectively near flat, reflecting the crosscurrents rather than a confident directional call either way.
